content.full_text:
State of California
Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. BOX 807 · SACRAMENTO, 95804 · · · 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814
Technical Assistance/Administration · · · Executive Legal · · · Enforcement · · · Conflict of Interest
(916) 322 5660         322 5901         322 6441         322 6644

March 12, 1979

Robert Livengood
1594 Adams Street, Apt. 2
Milpitas, CA 95035

Dear Mr. Livengood:

     This is to confirm the advice which I provided to you in
our telephone conversation of February 27, 1979. This advice
is provided to you pursuant to Section 83114 of the Political
Reform Act.1/ The facts as I understand them from our conver-
sation are as follows.

     You are a member of the Milpitas City Council. Although
you own a home and do intend to live in it for an indefinite
period of time, you are temporarily living outside of it in
a rented apartment. Approximately forty to forty-five percent
of all Milpitas residents live in rental units. You want to
know if your current status as a renter would prohibit you
from sponsoring a rent relief ordinance of some kind or
participating in the consideration of such an ordinance if
it were sponsored by another councilmember.

     Section 87100 of the Political Reform Act provides that:

     No public official at any level or state or local
government shall make, participate in making or
in any way attempt to use his official position
to influence a governmental decision in which he
knows or has reason to know he has a financial
interest.

___________________________
1/  All statutory references are to the Government Code unless
otherwise noted.

Mr. Robert Livungood
March 12, 1979
Page Two

Section 87103 provides that:

An official has a financial interest in a decision
within the meaning of Section 87100 if it is
reasonably foreseeable that the decision will
have a material financial effect, distinguishable
from its effect on the public generally, on:

(a) Any business entity in which the public
official has a direct or indirect investment worth
more than one thousand dollars ($1,000);

(b) Any real property in which the public
official has a direct or indirect interest worth
more than one thousand dollars ($1,000);

(c) Any source of income, other than loans
by a commercial lending institution in the regular
course of business, aggregating two hundred fifty
dollars ($250) or more in value received by or
promised to the public official within twelve months
prior to the time when the decision is made; or

(d) Any business entity in which the public
official is a director, officer, partner, trustee,
employee, or holds any position of management.

By regulation, the Commission has determined that "A material
financial effect of a governmental decision on an official's interests...is distinguishable from its effect on the public
generally unless the decision will affect the official's interest
in substantially the same manner as it will affect all members of
the public or a significant segment of the public...." 2 Cal.
Adm. Code section 13703.

In the Ferraro opinion, 4 FPPC Ops. 62 (No. 78-009,
Nov. 7, 1978), the Commission considered the criteria which
should be used to determine whether any group is a "significant
segment of the public." Interestingly, that opinion also concerned a rent control measure, but the question at issue in it
was whether landlords, not renters, constituted a significant
segment of the public. In reaching its conclusion in the Ferraro
opinion, the Commission stated that "In order to be considered
a significant segment of the public, we think a group usually
must be large in numbers and heterogeneous in quality."
4 FPPC Ops. at p. 67. In other words, as they said later in

Mr. Robert Livengood
March 12, 1979
Page Three


the same opinion, the group should have a diverse nature and
a lack of group identity.

As they constitute forty to forty-five percent of all the
people in Milpitas, renters must certainly be a group large
in numbers within your jurisdiction. In addition, there are
no facts which would refute the assumption that renters in
Milpitas, like renters elsewhere in California, represent
virtually every occupation and interest group. In short,
renters must be considered a significant segment of the public
in Milpitas. Therefore, as long as there are no circumstances
peculiar to your situation which would cause a rent relief
measure to affect you in a different manner than other renters,
you may participate in the Council's consideration of a rent
relief measure, even to the extent of sponsoring one.

In closing, let me add that although the above conclusion
rests on the fact that renters make up such a large proportion
of all Milpitas residents, the conclusion would not necessarily
have been different if renters were only a small percentage
of all those who live in Milpitas. As set forth above, Section
87103 provides that an official has a financial interest in a
decision if it will affect a business entity in which he has
an investment or some position, real property in which he has
an interest, or a source of income of $250 or more in the past
year. Section 82033 of the Act, however, excludes from the term
"interest in real property" any leasehold interest of less
than ten years. Therefore, unless a rent measure would affect
a source of income to an official or a business entity to which
he had some connection, the official would not be disqualified
unless his lease was longer than 10 years.

If you have any further questions concerning the Political
Reform Act, please do not hesitate to call us.

Sincerely,

Sarah T. Cameron
Counsel
Conflicts of Interest Division

State of California

Fair Political Practices Commission

P.O. BOX 307 · SACRAMENTO, 95804 · · · 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814

[illegible text at top]

APRIL 12, 1979

A-09-119

Assemblyman Stan Statham
P.O. Box 1001
Chico, CA 95927

Dear Mr. Statham:

Thank you for your letter dated March 11, 1979, regarding
the conflict of interest provisions of the Political Reform
Act.

The facts as I understand them are as follows. During 1978,
you participated in an Assembly vote that would allow individuals
to supply for site funds in order to operate half-way houses
for mentally ill patients. Recently, you were approached
by an individual wishing to buy a piece of real property owned
by you and your spouse. It is your understanding that this
individual plans to contribute a half-way house for the mentally
ill on the property, and to seek financial assistance under
the funding program recently adopted by the Legislature. Your
wife stated, during a March 13 telephone conversation with me,
that there had been no contact or communication with this
potential buyer regarding the property until after your par-
ticipation in the vote to fund half-way houses. You have
asked us whether it is a conflict of interest for you to sell
your property to this individual because of his intention to
establish a half-way house.

Government Code Section 8100[1/ states that,

No public official at any level of state or
local government shall make, participate in
making or in any way attempt to use his official
position to influence a governmental decision
in which he knows of the vision to know he has
a financial interest.
(Emphasis added.)

1/ All statutory references are to the Government Code unless
otherwise noted.

Mr. Stan Statham
April 12, 1979
Page Two


Thus the Act requires you to refrain from making or participating
in the making of governmental decisions in which you are financially interested.

You are not making or participating in the making of a
governmental decision when selling interests in real property
in your private capacity. Therefore, the Act does not impose
any restrictions on you with respect to the sale of your
property. However, when the property is sold, the buyer will
become a source of income to you and consequently, the Act
requires you to refrain from participating in all subsequent
governmental decisions which may materially affect the buyer.
See Government Code Sections 87100 and 87103(a).

I hope that my advice is helpful. If you have any questions
regarding this matter, please do not hesitate to contact me.

Sincerely,

[signature]

Dwight E. Dickerson
Local Counsel
Conflicts of Interest Division

DE:nrw

Stan
Statham
ASSEMBLYMAN

Post Office Box 1001 - Chico, California 95927

MARCH 13, 1979

FPPC
P.O. 807
SACRAMENTO, CA. 95304

GENTLEMEN:

I WOULD LIKE A WRITTEN OPINION FOR A POSSIBLE CONFLICT OF INTEREST
BASED ON THE FOLLOWING FACTS.

AB3052 PASSED THE ASSEMBLY LAST YEAR ON A 70-2 VOTE. I VOTED YES.
IT IS CHAPTERED 1233 (1978). IT DEALS WITH 3 MILLION DOLLARS WHEREBY
YOU CAN APPLY FOR THIS MONEY FOR FUNDING OF HALF-WAY HOUSES FOR
MENTALLY ILL PATIENTS.

MY WIFE WAS RECENTLY APPROACHED TO SELL A PIECE OF PROPERTY THAT WE
OWN AT 1263 ESPLANADE, CHICO TO SOMEONE THAT WILL BE APPLYING FOR
FUNDING FROM THIS BILL.

THE PROPERTY HAS NOT BEEN SOLD AS YET, BUT WE EXPECT TO ENTER INTO
A WRITTEN CONTRACT TO SELL SOON, WITH A CONTINGENCY THAT WOULD ALLOW
US TO WITHDRAW, IF IN FACT IT IS A CONFLICT.

IF YOU NEED ADDITIONAL INFORMATION, PLEASE CALL MY WIFE, MRS. LOVIE
STATHAM: (916) 342-7800.

SINCERELY,

Stan Statham

STAN STATHAM

SS:LS

Paid by: STAN STATHAM for Assembly · P.O. Box 1001 · Chico

sections.question_synthetic: The document contains two separate advice matters: (1) whether a Milpitas City Council member who is temporarily renting can sponsor or participate in consideration of a rent relief ordinance, and (2) whether an Assemblyman can sell property to a buyer who intends to establish a halfway house funded under legislation the Assemblyman voted for.
sections.conclusion_synthetic: The FPPC concluded that the City Council member may participate in and sponsor rent relief ordinances because renters constitute a significant segment of the public in Milpitas (40-45%) and the measure would affect the official in substantially the same manner as other renters. Regarding the Assemblyman's property sale, the FPPC determined that the sale itself does not violate conflict of interest provisions, but once the property is sold, the buyer becomes a source of income and the official must recuse from subsequent governmental decisions materially affecting the buyer.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: Document contains two separate advice letters combined (Livengood from March 12, 1979 and Statham from April 12, 1979), plus the incoming request letter from Statham dated March 13, 1979. No traditional 'QUESTION' and 'CONCLUSION' section headers present; structured as narrative letters. Both are FPPC responses to inquiries about conflict of interest under Political Reform Act. High confidence in extraction despite lack of standard formatting.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: The document contains two separate advice matters: (1) whether a Milpitas City Council member who is temporarily renting can sponsor or participate in consideration of a rent relief ordinance, and (2) whether an Assemblyman can sell property to a buyer who intends to establish a halfway house funded under legislation the Assemblyman voted for.

CONCLUSION: The FPPC concluded that the City Council member may participate in and sponsor rent relief ordinances because renters constitute a significant segment of the public in Milpitas (40-45%) and the measure would affect the official in substantially the same manner as other renters. Regarding the Assemblyman's property sale, the FPPC determined that the sale itself does not violate conflict of interest provisions, but once the property is sold, the buyer becomes a source of income and the official must recuse from subsequent governmental decisions materially affecting the buyer.

embedding.summary: Two FPPC advice letters address conflict of interest questions: one regarding a council member's participation in rent relief legislation, and another regarding an assemblyman's sale of property to a buyer seeking to establish a halfway house using state funds. Both conclude that participation is permissible under certain circumstances based on the Political Reform Act's provisions.
